facts.text: R.E.M. is widely credited as one of the first alternative rock bands to achieve mainstream commercial success in the 1980s.
The term 'indie' originally referred to music released on independent record labels rather than major ones.
Shoegaze got its name because performers in the genre often stared at their effects pedals on stage, appearing to gaze at their shoes.
Britpop emerged in the early 1990s as a reaction against American grunge and was led by bands like Oasis, Blur, and Pulp.
The Pixies' quiet-loud-quiet song dynamic directly influenced grunge and countless alternative rock bands.
College radio stations in the 1980s were crucial for breaking alternative bands that received no mainstream airplay.
Sub Pop Records, founded in Seattle in 1988, became one of the most influential indie labels by signing early grunge acts.
Radiohead's 'OK Computer' is frequently cited as one of the greatest albums of all time across multiple publications.
The Smiths' Morrissey became one of alternative music's most iconic and controversial frontmen during the 1980s.
Matador Records was co-founded by Chris Lombardi in 1989 and became home to Pavement, Guided by Voices, and Yo La Tengo.
Post-rock is a subgenre that uses rock instrumentation but emphasizes texture and atmosphere over traditional song structure.
Sonic Youth was known for using unconventional guitar tunings, sometimes using up to 60 different tunings on a single tour.
The term 'alternative rock' was popularized in the 1980s by college radio DJs and music journalists.
4AD Records, founded in London in 1980, became known for its ethereal and atmospheric roster including Cocteau Twins and Dead Can Dance.
Rough Trade Records was founded in 1978 and played a pivotal role in the UK independent music scene.
Merge Records was co-founded by Mac McCaughan and Laura Ballance of Superchunk in 1989 in Chapel Hill, North Carolina.
Bon Iver's debut album 'For Emma, Forever Ago' was recorded in a remote Wisconsin hunting cabin during the winter.
The Strokes' debut album 'Is This It' is credited with sparking the garage rock revival of the early 2000s.
Arcade Fire won the Grammy for Album of the Year in 2011 for 'The Suburbs,' shocking many in the mainstream music world.
Tame Impala is essentially a solo project; Kevin Parker writes, records, produces, and performs most of the music himself.
Vampire Weekend's self-titled debut album drew from Afropop influences, which was unusual for an indie rock band at the time.
Lo-fi music intentionally uses low-fidelity recording techniques as an aesthetic choice rather than a limitation.
My Bloody Valentine's 'Loveless' nearly bankrupted Creation Records due to its lengthy and expensive recording process.
Pavement's Stephen Malkmus became an icon of 1990s slacker culture and indie rock.
Modest Mouse's 'Float On' became one of the biggest indie crossover hits when it reached the Billboard Hot 100 in 2004.
Elliott Smith was nominated for an Academy Award for his song 'Miss Misery' from the Good Will Hunting soundtrack.
Jeff Buckley's 'Grace' sold poorly upon release but became one of the most acclaimed albums of the 1990s posthumously.
Wilco's 'Yankee Hotel Foxtrot' was famously rejected by its label Reprise, then picked up by Nonesuch — both owned by Warner Bros.
The Jesus and Mary Chain were known for playing extremely short, feedback-drenched live sets that often incited audience riots.
Guided by Voices released over 100 albums and EPs, many recorded on four-track tape machines in Robert Pollard's basement.
Slowdive was dropped by Creation Records in the mid-1990s, only to reunite in 2014 to widespread acclaim.
The National's Matt Berninger worked in advertising before the band's breakthrough album 'Boxer' in 2007.
Sufjan Stevens originally planned to record an album for each of the 50 U.S. states but abandoned the project after two.
Death Cab for Cutie took their name from a song by the Bonzo Dog Doo-Dah Band.
Neutral Milk Hotel's 'In the Aeroplane Over the Sea' was partly inspired by the diary of Anne Frank.
Dinosaur Jr.'s J Mascis is known for playing at extremely high volumes, reportedly using Marshall stacks turned up to their maximum.
The Replacements were known for chaotic, sometimes drunken live performances that became legendary in punk and alt-rock circles.
Hüsker Dü's 'Zen Arcade' was one of the first hardcore punk albums to incorporate melodic songwriting, influencing alternative rock.
Yo La Tengo has released over 15 studio albums since forming in Hoboken, New Jersey, in 1984.
Belle and Sebastian formed in Glasgow in 1996 and originally released their debut album as a music business school project.
Cat Power's Chan Marshall overcame severe stage fright that caused her to perform with her back to the audience in early shows.
Sleater-Kinney was named after a road in Lacey, Washington, near where the band's members lived.
The Breeders' 'Cannonball' became one of the biggest alternative hits of 1993, driven by heavy MTV rotation.
Liz Phair's 'Exile in Guyville' was conceived as a song-by-song response to the Rolling Stones' 'Exile on Main St.'
Sigur Rós sings in a mix of Icelandic and a made-up language called 'Hopelandic' or 'Vonlenska.'
Mogwai named their band after the creatures from the 1984 film 'Gremlins.'
Explosions in the Sky gained wider fame when their music was used in the TV series 'Friday Night Lights.'
Godspeed You! Black Emperor is a Canadian post-rock collective that has featured up to 20 members at various points.
The Cure's Robert Smith has maintained his signature look of smeared lipstick and wild hair for over four decades.
Joy Division's Ian Curtis suffered from epilepsy, and his condition deeply influenced the band's intense and dark lyrical themes.
New Order formed from the remaining members of Joy Division and pioneered the fusion of post-punk and electronic music.
The Stone Roses' self-titled debut album is considered one of the defining records of the late 1980s UK indie scene.
Mazzy Star's 'Fade into You' became an unexpected mainstream hit in 1994 despite the band's reclusive nature.
Ride was one of the original shoegaze bands and reunited in 2014 after an 18-year hiatus.
Cocteau Twins' Elizabeth Fraser sang in a style so abstract that many listeners believed she was singing in a foreign language.
Sebadoh's Lou Barlow was fired from Dinosaur Jr. in 1989, leading him to focus on the lo-fi pioneer project full-time.
Interpol's debut album 'Turn on the Bright Lights' drew heavy comparisons to Joy Division upon its release in 2002.
LCD Soundsystem's James Murphy co-founded DFA Records, which became an influential indie dance-punk label.
TV on the Radio blended indie rock with electronic production, doo-wop, and free jazz influences.
Animal Collective's 'Merriweather Post Pavilion' brought experimental pop to a wider audience when it was released in 2009.
Fleet Foxes' self-titled debut was praised for its lush vocal harmonies, drawing comparisons to Crosby, Stills & Nash.
Beach House's dream pop sound relies heavily on Victoria Legrand's organ playing and Alex Scally's atmospheric guitar work.
St. Vincent's Annie Clark is known for her angular guitar style and theatrically designed custom Ernie Ball Music Man guitars.
Phoebe Bridgers has been credited with helping to popularize a new wave of indie folk and emo-inflected songwriting.
Weezer's 'Pinkerton' was a commercial failure on release in 1996 but later became a cult classic and critical favorite.
The Cranberries' Dolores O'Riordan wrote 'Zombie' as a protest against the 1993 IRA bombing in Warrington, England.
Bjork began her career in the Icelandic alternative band The Sugarcubes before launching her acclaimed solo career.
PJ Harvey is the only artist to have won the Mercury Prize twice.
Fiona Apple's debut album 'Tidal' was released when she was just 18, and it went platinum within a year.
Spoon has been called one of the most consistent indie rock bands of the 2000s and 2010s by numerous critics.
The War on Drugs' 'Lost in the Dream' was inspired by frontman Adam Granduciel's struggles with anxiety and depression.
Courtney Barnett's conversational, deadpan vocal delivery style has drawn comparisons to Lou Reed and Bob Dylan.
Japanese Breakfast's Michelle Zauner is also a bestselling author, having written the memoir 'Crying in H Mart.'
Mitski's 'Puberty 2' is frequently cited as one of the best indie rock albums of the 2010s.
Soccer Mommy's Sophie Allison began uploading songs to Bandcamp as a teenager in Nashville.
Snail Mail's Lindsey Jordan was only 18 when she released her critically acclaimed debut album 'Lush' in 2018.
The DIY ethos of indie music encourages artists to self-produce, self-release, and self-promote their work without major label support.
Bandcamp became a vital platform for independent musicians, allowing direct-to-fan sales with artists keeping most of the revenue.
Zine culture was closely tied to the indie music scene in the 1980s and 1990s, with fanzines promoting underground bands.
KEXP in Seattle is one of the most influential independent radio stations for breaking new alternative and indie music.
Pitchfork Media, founded in 1996, became one of the most influential publications for indie music criticism.
Touch and Go Records, based in Chicago, released records by Shellac, The Jesus Lizard, and Slint without artist contracts.
Dischord Records, founded by Ian MacKaye of Fugazi, operated on a strict low-price, no-contract, DIY business model.
K Records, co-founded by Calvin Johnson in Olympia, Washington, was a hub for the lo-fi and riot grrrl movements.
Kill Rock Stars label signed Bikini Kill, Sleater-Kinney, and Elliott Smith, becoming a pillar of Pacific Northwest indie music.
Drag City Records has been home to Silver Jews, Bill Callahan, Joanna Newsom, and Ty Segall.
Jagjaguwar Records, based in Bloomington, Indiana, is the label home to Bon Iver, Angel Olsen, and Dinosaur Jr.
Domino Recording Company signed Arctic Monkeys and Franz Ferdinand, bridging the UK indie and mainstream worlds.
Saddle Creek Records grew out of Omaha, Nebraska's music scene and launched the careers of Bright Eyes and Cursive.
Slint's 'Spiderland' is considered a foundational post-rock album despite selling only a few thousand copies on its 1991 release.
Talk Talk's later albums 'Spirit of Eden' and 'Laughing Stock' departed from synth-pop to help invent post-rock.
Tortoise is one of the earliest and most well-known post-rock bands from the Chicago scene of the 1990s.
Mono, a Japanese post-rock band, is known for pairing crushing crescendos with delicate quiet passages in their live performances.
Broken Social Scene often has more than a dozen members performing on stage, drawing from Toronto's indie music community.
The Olivia Tremor Control was a key band in the Elephant 6 Recording Company collective alongside Neutral Milk Hotel.
The Elephant 6 Recording Company was a loose collective of bands based primarily in Athens, Georgia, in the 1990s.
Superchunk's 'No Pocky for Kitty' helped establish the indie rock sound of the early 1990s on Matador Records.
Throwing Muses, led by Kristin Hersh, were the first American band signed to 4AD Records.
The Breeders were formed by Pixies bassist Kim Deal and Throwing Muses guitarist Tanya Donelly as a side project.
Stereolab blended krautrock, lounge pop, and Marxist politics into their unique brand of indie music.
Shellac, Steve Albini's band, refused to sign to major labels and insisted on selling their albums at low prices.
Steve Albini recorded hundreds of influential indie and alternative albums, including records for Pixies and PJ Harvey.
Albini famously refused to take royalties for his recording work, charging only a flat studio fee.
The alternative rock boom of the early 1990s led major labels to sign hundreds of indie bands in a gold rush for the next big act.
Lollapalooza was founded in 1991 as a touring festival to showcase alternative music, arts, and culture.
Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival began in 1999 and has become one of the world's premiere events for indie and alternative music.
Glastonbury Festival in Somerset, England, has been featuring alternative and indie acts since the early 1980s.
Pitchfork Music Festival in Chicago has been one of the most important showcases for indie music since its inception in 2006.
Primavera Sound in Barcelona has grown into one of Europe's largest festivals with a strong indie and alternative lineup.
SXSW in Austin, Texas, combines a music festival with a film and interactive conference, and has broken many indie bands.
All Tomorrow's Parties was a curator-led festival concept where artists chose the entire lineup, championing experimental and indie music.
Reading and Leeds Festivals in England have been a summer staple for British alternative and indie music fans since the 1960s.
Iceland Airwaves in Reykjavik is a festival dedicated to showcasing Icelandic and international indie music each November.
Bandcamp Fridays, where the platform waives its revenue share, became a major event for supporting indie musicians.
The Shins' appearance in the film 'Garden State' led to an enormous boost in their popularity.
Bright Eyes' Conor Oberst was often called the 'new Bob Dylan' in the early 2000s for his poetic songwriting.
Iron & Wine's Sam Beam was a film professor at the University of Miami before his music career took off.
Feist's '1234' received massive exposure through an Apple iPod Nano commercial in 2007.
MGMT's 'Kids' became an inescapable song in 2008, appearing in commercials, films, and sports arenas worldwide.
Foster the People's 'Pumped Up Kicks' was one of the biggest indie crossover hits of 2011.
Gotye's 'Somebody That I Used to Know' went to number one worldwide in 2012, a rare feat for an indie artist.
Alt-J won the Mercury Prize for their debut album 'An Awesome Wave' in 2012.
Hozier's 'Take Me to Church' became a global hit in 2014, starting from an independent Irish release.
Mumford & Sons brought a banjo-driven indie folk sound to mainstream arenas in the early 2010s.
The Lumineers' 'Ho Hey' became one of the biggest indie folk crossover songs of 2012.
Mac DeMarco is known for his laid-back 'jizz jazz' style and irreverent, gap-toothed stage persona.
Car Seat Headrest's Will Toledo self-released 12 albums on Bandcamp before signing to Matador Records.
Alvvays' debut album features the jangly hit 'Archie, Marry Me,' which became an indie pop staple.
Angel Olsen evolved from sparse folk recordings to expansive indie rock on albums like 'My Woman' and 'All Mirrors.'
Weyes Blood's 'Titanic Rising' was hailed as a masterpiece of baroque pop and indie songwriting upon its 2019 release.
Fontaines D.C. brought a literary, poetry-influenced approach to post-punk revival from Dublin, Ireland.
Wet Leg's self-titled debut album won the Grammy for Best Alternative Music Album in 2023.
Boygenius, the supergroup of Phoebe Bridgers, Julien Baker, and Lucy Dacus, released a full album in 2023 after a 2018 EP.
The xx's minimalist sound, built on sparse guitar, bass, and quiet vocals, was highly influential in the 2010s indie scene.
Caribou's Dan Snaith holds a PhD in mathematics from Imperial College London.
Portishead's 'Dummy' helped define trip-hop and has been a touchstone for alternative and indie artists since 1994.
The term 'dream pop' was coined in the late 1980s to describe ethereal, atmospheric guitar-based music with soft vocals.
Swervedriver combined shoegaze textures with a harder-edged, more riff-driven approach in the early 1990s.
Nothing and Whirr carried the shoegaze sound into the 2010s as part of a revival sometimes called 'nu-gaze.'
Oasis and Blur's rivalry in the mid-1990s, known as the 'Battle of Britpop,' dominated UK tabloid headlines.
Pulp's Jarvis Cocker became a Britpop icon with the hit 'Common People,' which satirized class tourism.
Suede's debut album predated both Blur and Oasis in launching the Britpop movement in 1993.
Elastica's self-titled debut became the fastest-selling debut album in UK history at the time of its 1995 release.
The Verve's 'Bitter Sweet Symphony' sampled an orchestral version of a Rolling Stones song, leading to a protracted royalties dispute.
Manic Street Preachers are one of the few Welsh bands to achieve major success in the Britpop era.
Coldplay's debut 'Parachutes' emerged from the post-Britpop era and launched one of the biggest bands in the world.
Franz Ferdinand's angular, danceable indie rock helped ignite the post-punk revival of the early 2000s.
Bloc Party's 'Silent Alarm' was a landmark album of the mid-2000s post-punk revival in the UK.
The Libertines' chaotic career and Pete Doherty's tabloid presence made them central figures in 2000s UK indie rock.
Arctic Monkeys became the first band to reach number one in the UK primarily through internet hype and fan-distributed demos.
Foals evolved from math-rock influenced indie to arena-sized anthems over the course of their career.
Wolf Alice won the Mercury Prize twice, for 'Visions of a Life' in 2018 and 'Blue Weekend' in 2022.
Idles' aggressive post-punk tackles themes of masculinity, immigration, and class in modern Britain.
The 1975 blended indie rock with pop, electronic, and ambient elements, becoming one of the biggest UK bands of the 2010s.
R.E.M.'s Michael Stipe was one of the first alternative rock frontmen to use his platform for political activism.
R.E.M.'s 'Murmur' topped many critics' year-end lists in 1983 and helped define the jangly college rock sound.
R.E.M.'s 'Losing My Religion' featured a mandolin riff that became one of the most recognizable in pop music history.
The Pixies reunited in 2004 after an 11-year hiatus and have continued touring and releasing new music since.
Sonic Youth's 'Daydream Nation' was one of the first albums selected for preservation in the Library of Congress's National Recording Registry.
Sonic Youth's Thurston Moore and Kim Gordon were married for 27 years, and their divorce in 2011 effectively ended the band.
The Smiths released only four studio albums between 1984 and 1987 but remain one of the most influential bands in indie music history.
Johnny Marr of The Smiths is widely regarded as one of the greatest guitar players in the history of alternative rock.
Radiohead's decision to release 'In Rainbows' as a pay-what-you-want download in 2007 challenged traditional music industry models.
Radiohead's 'Kid A' marked a dramatic shift from guitar rock to electronic and experimental textures.
Modest Mouse's Isaac Brock wrote many of the band's early songs while living in his car in the Pacific Northwest.
Tame Impala's 'Currents' marked a shift from psychedelic rock to synth-driven pop, expanding their audience dramatically.
Vampire Weekend's Ezra Koenig studied at Columbia University, and the band's early songs reference campus life and preppy culture.
Arcade Fire's 'Funeral' was recorded partly in a church in Farnham, Quebec.
Arcade Fire's Win Butler and Régine Chassagne are married, and both are central members of the band.
The Strokes recorded their debut 'Is This It' with producer Gordon Raphael in a small New York City studio called Transporterraum.
The White Stripes' Jack White insisted the duo of just guitar and drums was all that was needed to make rock and roll.
Yeah Yeah Yeahs' Karen O became an icon of New York City's early 2000s indie rock scene with her wild stage presence.
Interpol formed at New York University in 1997 and became fixtures of the Lower East Side music scene.
Fleet Foxes' Robin Pecknold took a hiatus from music to attend Columbia University before returning to release 'Helplessness Blues.'
Joanna Newsom plays a 47-string concert harp and uses her unique vocal style as a signature of her indie folk sound.
The Postal Service, a side project of Death Cab for Cutie's Ben Gibbard and Dntel's Jimmy Tamborello, released only one studio album.
The Postal Service's 'Give Up' has sold over a million copies, making it one of Sub Pop's best-selling records.
Sub Pop Records originally started as a fanzine called 'Subterranean Pop' before evolving into a full record label.
Merge Records has remained fully independent and artist-friendly for over 35 years.
Polyvinyl Record Co., based in Illinois, has been an important indie label for artists like American Football and Japanese Breakfast.
American Football's self-titled debut became a cult classic of emo and indie rock years after its 1999 release.
Emo's origins lie in the mid-1980s Washington, D.C. hardcore scene, with bands like Rites of Spring and Embrace.
Sunny Day Real Estate is often cited as one of the most important early emo bands of the 1990s.
Fugazi's shows never cost more than five dollars, and the band refused to sell merchandise at concerts.
Fugazi's 'Repeater' is considered one of the greatest post-hardcore albums and sold over a million copies without major label support.
Minor Threat's Ian MacKaye coined the term 'straight edge,' which became a major movement in punk and alternative subcultures.
Chillwave, a microgenre of the late 2000s, featured lo-fi, synth-driven indie pop by artists like Washed Out, Toro y Moi, and Neon Indian.
Washed Out's 'Feel It All Around' was used as the theme song for the TV series 'Portlandia.'
Real Estate's jangly guitar pop evokes suburban nostalgia and has made them mainstays of 2010s indie rock.
Unknown Mortal Orchestra's Ruban Nielson is a New Zealand-born musician who blends psychedelic rock with lo-fi R&B.
King Gizzard & the Lizard Wizard released five studio albums in the year 2017 alone.
Ty Segall has released over 15 solo albums and numerous collaborative records, making him one of the most prolific figures in garage rock.
Parquet Courts combine post-punk energy with art-rock intellectualism, rooted in the Brooklyn DIY scene.
Protomartyr's Joe Casey delivers spoken-word-style vocals over dark, post-punk instrumentals, emerging from Detroit's indie scene.
Cloud Nothings' Dylan Baldi recorded the band's early albums entirely by himself in his parents' basement in Cleveland.
Japandroids' 'Celebration Rock' was hailed as one of the most exhilarating indie rock albums of 2012.
Screaming Females' Marissa Paternoster is regarded as one of the best guitarists in modern indie and punk rock.
Hop Along's Frances Quinlan has one of the most distinctive and expressive voices in contemporary indie rock.
Frankie Cosmos' Greta Kline released hundreds of songs on Bandcamp before signing to Sub Pop.
Jay Som's Melina Duterte recorded her breakthrough album 'Everybody Works' mostly in her bedroom.
Bedroom pop as a genre emerged from the accessibility of home recording software like GarageBand and Ableton Live.
Clairo's early songs were recorded in her bedroom and went viral on YouTube, making her a poster child for bedroom pop.
Khruangbin blends psychedelic rock with global music influences from Thai funk, to dub, to Middle Eastern melodies.
Men I Trust, from Montreal, craft dreamy indie pop with a lo-fi sheen that has amassed them a massive online following.
Cassette culture in the 1980s and 1990s allowed indie bands to cheaply produce and distribute their own music.
Vinyl on vinyl saw a resurgence in the 2010s, with indie labels leading the charge in pressing new records on the format.
Record Store Day, launched in 2007, became an annual celebration of independent record shops and exclusive vinyl releases.
College rock in the 1980s referred to bands that were primarily played on university radio stations, like R.E.M. and the Replacements.
120 Minutes on MTV was a crucial late-night program that exposed American audiences to alternative and indie music from 1986 to 2003.
The alternative rock format was officially recognized by Billboard with the creation of the Modern Rock Tracks chart in 1988.
NME and Melody Maker were the two main UK music weeklies that covered and shaped the indie and alternative music landscape.
Tiny Desk Concerts by NPR became one of the most popular platforms for indie musicians to reach new audiences.
Oasis's '(What's the Story) Morning Glory?' has sold over 22 million copies, making it one of the best-selling albums of all time.
Blur's Damon Albarn went on to create Gorillaz, one of the most successful virtual band projects in music history.
The phrase 'selling out' was a major concern in indie culture, where signing to a major label could damage a band's credibility.
Many consider Sonic Youth's signing to DGC Records in 1990 as helping open doors for alternative bands on major labels.
Mudhoney, one of the original Sub Pop grunge bands, has remained active and committed to the indie underground for over 35 years.
The lo-fi aesthetic gained a philosophical dimension — imperfection in sound was seen as more authentic than polished studio production.
Daniel Johnston was a beloved lo-fi and outsider musician whose handmade cassette tapes gained a cult following in Austin, Texas.
The Mountain Goats' John Darnielle recorded dozens of albums on a Panasonic boombox before transitioning to studio recordings.
Wilco's Jeff Tweedy has written a memoir and become an advocate for mental health and addiction recovery.
Jimmy Eat World's 'Bleed American' bridged the gap between indie rock and mainstream emo-pop in 2001.
Unwound, from Olympia, Washington, blended post-hardcore with noise rock and remain a cult favorite in indie circles.
Helmet's syncopated, heavy riffs on 'Meantime' influenced both alternative metal and indie rock in the early 1990s.
Jawbox signed to Atlantic Records from Dischord, sparking debates about indie credibility in the 1990s punk community.
WFMU in Jersey City, New Jersey, is the longest-running freeform radio station in the United States.
Deerhunter's Bradford Cox has Marfan syndrome, which he has openly discussed and which influenced his artistic output.
Lemonheads' Evan Dando brought indie rock to MTV with a hit cover of Simon & Garfunkel's 'Mrs. Robinson' in 1992.
The Flaming Lips are known for Wayne Coyne's elaborate stage shows involving giant plastic bubbles and confetti cannons.
Built to Spill's Doug Martsch is considered one of indie rock's greatest guitarists for his intricate, effects-laden solos.
Pedro the Lion's David Bazan wrote concept albums exploring faith, doubt, and morality in an indie rock context.
Nada Surf's 'Popular' was a novelty hit in 1996, but the band evolved into a respected indie rock act over the following decades.
At the Drive-In's 'Relationship of Command' was a highly intense post-hardcore album that influenced many indie and alternative bands.
The Mars Volta formed from the ashes of At the Drive-In and pushed progressive rock into indie circles.
GBV's Robert Pollard was a fourth-grade teacher in Dayton, Ohio, while simultaneously leading one of indie's most prolific bands.
Beat Happening, led by K Records' Calvin Johnson, pioneered the lo-fi, stripped-down sound of indie pop in the 1980s.
Pavement's 'Slanted and Enchanted' was recorded for approximately $800 and became one of the defining lo-fi indie albums.
The Afghan Whigs blended alternative rock with soul and R&B influences, which was uncommon in the early 1990s indie scene.
Andrew Bird is known for live looping his violin and whistling, creating intricate one-man arrangements on stage.
Black Midi is part of a new wave of experimental UK bands pushing the boundaries of indie and post-punk.
Spotify playlists like 'Indie Pop' and 'Alternative Hits' have become major drivers of discovery for independent artists.
Thee Oh Sees (now Osees) have released dozens of albums, helping to define the modern garage and psych rock scenes.
Steve Lacy of The Internet released his debut EP entirely recorded on his iPhone using GarageBand.
Waxahatchee's Katie Crutchfield emerged from the DIY punk house scene in Birmingham, Alabama.
Big Thief has released four studio albums in three years, a prolific output rate unusual for modern indie bands.
Passion Pit's Michael Angelakos has been open about his struggles with bipolar disorder and its influence on his music.
Best Coast's Bethany Cosentino brought California surf pop vibes to the indie rock world in the early 2010s.
FKA twigs' experimental approach to R&B and art pop has been embraced by the indie music community.
Four Tet's Kieran Hebden transitioned from post-rock with Fridge to become one of the most acclaimed electronic artists in indie circles.
Wild Nothing's 'Gemini' was a key album in the dream pop revival of the early 2010s.
Massive Attack's 'Mezzanine' was a genre-defining album that influenced alternative rock, trip-hop, and electronic music.
Catherine Wheel's 'Ferment' is considered one of the essential shoegaze albums of the early 1990s.
Rex Orange County blended indie pop with jazz and soul influences to become one of the biggest indie acts of the late 2010s.
Crumb's psychedelic indie pop is built around Lila Ramani's jazz-influenced guitar playing.
The indie sleaze movement of the late 2000s and early 2010s centered around vice-fueled nightlife in New York and London.
KCRW's 'Morning Becomes Eclectic' has been a top tastemaker radio show for indie and alternative music since the 1990s.
Of Montreal's Kevin Barnes is known for elaborate, theatrical live shows and prolific output, releasing over 17 studio albums.
Deerhoof has been described as one of the most creative and unpredictable bands in indie rock, spanning over 25 years.
Broadcast's Trish Keenan created a distinctive sound blending vintage electronics with 1960s pop aesthetics.
Radiohead's Thom Yorke has released several solo albums and formed the side project Atoms for Peace with Flea of the Red Hot Chili Peppers.
Archers of Loaf were one of the most popular indie rock bands of the 1990s Chapel Hill music scene.
Grizzly Bear's 'Veckatimest' featured orchestral arrangements recorded at churches and studios across multiple states.
TV on the Radio's Tunde Adebimpe is also a visual artist and actor who appeared in the film 'Rachel Getting Married.'
The Walkmen's 'The Rat' is one of the most anthemic indie rock songs of the 2000s.
Speedy Ortiz's Sadie Dupuis blends complex guitar work with confessional lyrics in the tradition of 1990s indie rock.
Garbage's Shirley Manson was one of the first female-fronted alternative rock acts to achieve mainstream 1990s success.
Veruca Salt's 'Seether' became an alternative rock radio staple in 1994.
The Vaselines were a short-lived Scottish indie pop duo whose songs were famously covered by members of major grunge bands.
Velocity Girl was a prominent shoegazing-influenced band from the Washington, D.C. area in the early 1990s.
Toro y Moi's Chaz Bear has explored funk, house, and R&B throughout his career while maintaining indie credibility.
Dry Cleaning's spoken-word vocal style over angular post-punk instrumentals has made them a standout act in UK indie.
Shame emerged from the same South London scene as Black Midi and represents the new wave of UK post-punk.
Camp Cope, from Melbourne, Australia, became prominent voices in indie rock's conversation about gender equity and safe spaces at shows.
James Blake's self-titled debut blurred the lines between electronic music, dubstep, and indie singer-songwriter traditions.
The Numero Group is a reissue label that has unearthed forgotten indie, soul, gospel, and punk recordings.
The NME Awards and the BRIT Awards have categories specifically recognizing alternative and indie music achievements.
Melody Maker ceased publication in 2000, merging with NME, which itself went online-only in 2018.
Pearl Jam's battle against Ticketmaster in the mid-1990s highlighted issues of ticket monopolies that affected indie and alternative touring.
Echo & the Bunnymen's 'The Killing Moon' has been called one of the greatest songs of the 1980s by multiple critics.
Quicksand's 'Slip' is regarded as a landmark post-hardcore album that influenced many 1990s alternative rock bands.
Sub Pop's 'Singles Club' subscription service in the late 1980s was an innovative way to build a fanbase for new artists.
Bjork once physically attacked a reporter at a Bangkok airport in one of the most famous celebrity altercations caught on camera.
DIIV's Zachary Cole Smith created a modern shoegaze sound heavily influenced by Joy Division and My Bloody Valentine.
Lush were key members of the early 1990s shoegaze movement alongside My Bloody Valentine and Slowdive.
The Get Up Kids' 'Something to Write Home About' is considered one of the defining emo albums of the late 1990s.
Sub Pop's marketing strategy in the late 1980s included hyping the Seattle scene to UK press, helping create the grunge phenomenon.
My Bloody Valentine's Kevin Shields reportedly spent over two years and £250,000 recording 'Loveless.'
Radiohead considered breaking up after the grueling 'OK Computer' tour before deciding to radically reinvent their sound.
Arcade Fire's debut album 'Funeral' was named after several band members' relatives who passed away during its recording.
The Strokes' Julian Casablancas is the son of John Casablancas, the founder of Elite Model Management.
Elliott Smith played his Oscar-nominated song 'Miss Misery' live at the 1998 Academy Awards ceremony, appearing visibly nervous among Hollywood stars.
Neutral Milk Hotel's Jeff Mangum became so reclusive after 'In the Aeroplane Over the Sea' that fans speculated he had died.
The Flaming Lips once held a Guinness World Record for the most live concerts in 24 hours, performing eight shows in different cities.
Pavement almost signed to major label Capitol Records before Stephen Malkmus got cold feet and stayed with Matador.
The Pixies broke up via fax in 1993 — Black Francis sent the other members a fax informing them the band was over.
Bon Iver's Justin Vernon chose the name 'Bon Iver' from the French phrase 'bon hiver,' meaning 'good winter.'
Modest Mouse's 'Float On' was partly inspired by Isaac Brock's near-death experience in a car accident.
Vampire Weekend recorded parts of their debut album in a dorm room at Columbia University.
Tame Impala's Kevin Parker produced and played every instrument on the band's first two albums entirely by himself.
Arctic Monkeys' debut album 'Whatever People Say I Am, That's What I'm Not' became the fastest-selling debut in UK chart history in 2006.
The White Stripes claimed to be brother and sister, but they were actually a divorced married couple.
Sufjan Stevens' 'Illinois' was originally released with artwork featuring Superman, which had to be changed after DC Comics threatened legal action.
Death Cab for Cutie's name comes from a song performed by the Bonzo Dog Band in the Beatles' film 'Magical Mystery Tour.'
The Cure's 'Disintegration' was written while Robert Smith was going through a deep personal crisis around his 30th birthday.
Joy Division's name was taken from a term used in a World War II concentration camp novel, 'House of Dolls.'
The Stone Roses' 'Second Coming' took five years to record, partially because the band spent extensive time playing video games in the studio.
Pulp existed for 17 years and released four albums before 'Common People' finally made them stars in 1995.
Oasis' Liam and Noel Gallagher have had a public feud for decades, and their breakup in 2009 occurred backstage at a Paris festival.
The Verve's Richard Ashcroft had to give up all royalties for 'Bitter Sweet Symphony' to the Rolling Stones' publisher, though they were later returned in 2019.
Sigur Rós' album '( )' has no song titles and no lyrics in any real language, yet is one of the most emotionally powerful post-rock records.
Mogwai once named a song 'Blur Are Shite' as a tongue-in-cheek jab during the peak of Britpop.
Godspeed You! Black Emperor's album 'Lift Your Skinny Fists Like Antennas to Heaven' has only four tracks but runs nearly 90 minutes.
LCD Soundsystem held a farewell concert at Madison Square Garden in 2011 that lasted nearly four hours, only to reunite five years later.
R.E.M.'s Bill Berry suffered a brain aneurysm on stage in 1995 in Lausanne, Switzerland, but made a full recovery.
Jeff Buckley drowned in the Wolf River Harbor in Memphis in 1997 while wading in fully clothed and singing Led Zeppelin's 'Whole Lotta Love.'
Sonic Youth's album 'EVOL' is 'LOVE' spelled backward.
Weezer's Rivers Cuomo enrolled at Harvard University between the band's debut and 'Pinkerton,' studying English composition.
PJ Harvey recorded her album 'Rid of Me' with Steve Albini in just two weeks at Pachyderm Studio in Minnesota.
The Replacements' Paul Westerberg once deliberately played an entire Saturday Night Live performance badly as an act of defiance.
Dinosaur Jr. originally went by 'Dinosaur' but were forced to add 'Jr.' after the 1970s supergroup Dinosaur threatened legal action.
Guided by Voices' Robert Pollard has written over 2,500 songs, making him one of the most prolific songwriters in rock history.
Slowdive were so poorly received during the Britpop era that a Melody Maker journalist once gave their album a one-word review: 'Naff.'
The Jesus and Mary Chain's 1985 debut gig at the North London Polytechnic lasted only fifteen minutes and ended in a riot.
Fiona Apple once gave an acceptance speech at the MTV VMAs calling the world 'bullshit,' which became a defining moment of 1990s counterculture.
Wilco streamed 'Yankee Hotel Foxtrot' for free on their website before it was officially released, and it still debuted at number 13 on the Billboard 200.
Daniel Johnston's most famous artwork, the 'Hi, How Are You' mural in Austin, Texas, has become a protected city landmark.
Cat Power's Chan Marshall adopted her stage name as a teenager and has said it was inspired by a trucker hat she found.
Beach House recorded their breakthrough album 'Teen Dream' in a church in upstate New York.
Cocteau Twins' 'Heaven or Las Vegas' was recorded while Elizabeth Fraser was pregnant and dealing with intense personal turmoil.
Sleater-Kinney's Carrie Brownstein co-created and starred in the IFC comedy series 'Portlandia' with Fred Armisen.
Fugazi maintained a meticulous archive of nearly every live show they ever played, with recordings available through Dischord Records.
Spoon's 'Girls Can Tell' was recorded partly with money from a legal settlement after Elektra Records dropped the band.
Mazzy Star's Hope Sandoval is famously reclusive and typically performs in near-total darkness to avoid the spotlight.
Black Francis of the Pixies enrolled in a student exchange program to Puerto Rico and the experience directly inspired several songs on 'Surfer Rosa.'
The term 'Madchester' described the late-1980s Manchester scene blending indie rock with acid house, led by the Happy Mondays and Stone Roses.
Ride's Andy Bell later became the bassist for Oasis, bridging the shoegaze and Britpop worlds.
King Gizzard & the Lizard Wizard released an album called 'Polygondwanaland' and made it available for anyone to press on vinyl for free.
Coachella's first edition in 1999 was a financial disaster, and organizers nearly abandoned the festival before trying again in 2001.
Blur's 'Parklife' was originally titled 'Soft Porn' before the band and label decided on a more radio-friendly name.
The Smiths' 'The Queen Is Dead' was recorded partly in Wayne's Brewery in Manchester because Morrissey wanted an unusual acoustic space.
Stereolab's name was taken from a subdivision of Vanguard Records that specialized in hi-fi test recordings.
Les Savy Fav's Tim Harrington is known for some of indie rock's most outrageous live performances, often stripping down to his underwear.
Deerhunter's Bradford Cox once played a solo show consisting of a single 30-minute drone, causing most of the audience to leave.
Built to Spill's Doug Martsch turned down offers from major labels multiple times to remain on Warner Bros.' indie-friendly imprint.
Bon Iver's use of Auto-Tune on the album '22, A Million' surprised fans who expected another folk-oriented record.
R.E.M. released their debut single 'Radio Free Europe' on the tiny Hib-Tone label, pressing only 1,000 copies.
The Smiths played their first live gig at the Ritz in Manchester on October 4, 1982.
Joy Division's Ian Curtis died on May 18, 1980, the day before the band was to embark on their first American tour.
Radiohead released 'OK Computer' on June 16, 1997, to immediate critical acclaim.
Radiohead released 'In Rainbows' as a pay-what-you-want download on October 10, 2007.
The Pixies released their debut full-length album 'Surfer Rosa' on March 21, 1988.
My Bloody Valentine released 'Loveless' on November 4, 1991, after years of painstaking recording.
R.E.M. released 'Automatic for the People' on October 5, 1992.
Arcade Fire released 'Funeral' on September 14, 2004, launching them to international acclaim.
The Strokes released 'Is This It' in the US on October 9, 2001, just weeks after the September 11 attacks.
Oasis released '(What's the Story) Morning Glory?' on October 2, 1995.
Blur and Oasis released competing singles on August 14, 1995, in the famous 'Battle of Britpop.'
Jeff Buckley released his only completed studio album 'Grace' on August 23, 1994.
Neutral Milk Hotel released 'In the Aeroplane Over the Sea' on February 10, 1998.
Elliott Smith died on October 21, 2003, from stab wounds in what was officially ruled an undetermined cause of death.
Sonic Youth released 'Daydream Nation' on October 18, 1988, a double album that defined alternative rock's artistic ambitions.
The Stone Roses released their self-titled debut on May 2, 1989.
The Cure released 'Disintegration' on May 2, 1989, the same day as The Stone Roses' debut.
Slint released 'Spiderland' on March 27, 1991, an album that grew into a post-rock touchstone.
Modest Mouse released 'The Lonesome Crowded West' on November 18, 1997.
Wilco released 'Yankee Hotel Foxtrot' on April 23, 2002, after streaming it on their website for months.
The White Stripes released 'Elephant' on April 1, 2003, featuring the massive hit 'Seven Nation Army.'
Bon Iver released 'For Emma, Forever Ago' on February 19, 2008, on the Jagjaguwar label.
Tame Impala released 'Currents' on July 17, 2015, marking a major evolution in their sound.
The Smiths released 'The Queen Is Dead' on June 16, 1986, considered by many their masterpiece.
Pavement released 'Slanted and Enchanted' on April 20, 1992, a landmark lo-fi indie rock album.
Sub Pop Records was officially incorporated on November 1, 1988.
Coachella held its first festival on October 9, 1999, headlined by Beck, Rage Against the Machine, and Tool.
Glastonbury Festival was first held on September 19, 1970, the day after Jimi Hendrix died, with tickets costing £1.
The first Pitchfork Music Festival was held on July 29, 2006, in Chicago's Union Park.
The first Lollapalooza festival toured the United States beginning on July 18, 1991.
Vampire Weekend released their self-titled debut album on January 29, 2008.
LCD Soundsystem played their farewell show at Madison Square Garden on April 2, 2011.
Arctic Monkeys released their debut album 'Whatever People Say I Am, That's What I'm Not' on January 23, 2006.
Arcade Fire won the Grammy for Album of the Year for 'The Suburbs' on February 13, 2011.
